title: The Ghost At The Second Bridge
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You'd call the man a senseless fool,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A blockhead or an ass,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whoן¿½d dare to say he saw the ghost
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Mount Victoria Pass;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But I believe the ghost is there,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For, if my eyes are right,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I saw it once upon a neן¿½er-
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To-be-forgotten night.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas in the year of eighty-nine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The day was nearly gone,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The stars were shining, and the moon
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is mentioned further on;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½d tramped as far as Hartley Vale,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thoן¿½ tired at the start,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But coming back I got a lift
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In Johnny Jonesן¿½s cart.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas winter on the mountains then,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The air was rather chill,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And so we stopped beside the inn
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That stands below the hill.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A fire was burning in the bar,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Johnny thought a glass
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Would give the tired horse a spell
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And help us up the Pass.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then Jimmy Bent came riding up,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A tidy chap was Jim,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He shouted twice, and so of course
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We had to shout for him.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And when at last we said good-night
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He bet a vulgar quid
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That we would see the ן¿½ghost in blackן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And sure enough we did.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And as we climbed the stony pinch
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Below the Camel Bridge,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We talked about the ן¿½Girl in blackן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who haunts the Second Bridge.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We reached the fence that guards the cliff
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And passed the corner post,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Johnny like a senseless fool
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Kept harping on the ghost.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Sheן¿½ll cross the moonlit road in haste
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And vanish down the track;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her long black hair hangs to her waist
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And she is dressed in black;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her face is white, a dull dead white,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her eyes are opened wide,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She never looks to left or right,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or turns to either side.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I didnן¿½t bן¿½lieve in ghosts at all,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thoן¿½ I was rather young,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But still I wished with all my heart
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That Jack would hold his tongue.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The time and place, as you will say,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (ן¿½Twas twelve oן¿½clock almost),
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Were both historically fa-
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vourable for a ghost.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But have you seen the Second Bridge
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Beneath the ן¿½Camelן¿½s Backן¿½?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It fills a gap that broke the ridge
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When convicts made the track;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And oן¿½er the right old Hartley Vale
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In homely beauty lies,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And oן¿½er the left the mighty walls
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Mount Victoria rise.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And thereן¿½s a spot above the bridge,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Just where the track is steep,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From which poor Convict Govett rode
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To christen Govettן¿½s Leap;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And here a teamster killed his wife,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For those old days were rough,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And here a dozen others had
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Been murdered, right enough.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The lonely moon was over all
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And she was shining well,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At angles from the sandstone wall
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The shifting moonbeams fell.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In short, the shifting moonbeams beamed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The air was still as death,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Save when the listening silence seemed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To speak beneath its breath.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The tangled bushes were not stirred
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Because there was no wind,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But now and then I thought I heard
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A startling noise behind.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then Johnny Jones began to quake;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His face was like the dead.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Donן¿½t look behind, for heavenן¿½s sake!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The ghost is there!ן¿½ he said.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He stared ahead, his eyes were fixed;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He whipped the horse like mad.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½You fool!ן¿½ I cried, ן¿½youן¿½re only mixed;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A drop too much youן¿½ve had.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ll never see a ghost, I swear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But I will find the cause.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I turned to see if it was there,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And sure enough it was!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Its look appeared to plead for aid
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (As far as I could see),
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Its hands were on the tailboard laid,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Its eyes were fixed on me.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The face, it cannot be denied
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was white, a dull dead white,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The great black eyes were opened wide
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And glistened in the light.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I stared at Jack; he stared ahead
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And madly plied the lash.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To show I wasnן¿½t scared, I said,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Why, Jack, weן¿½ve made a mash.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I tried to laugh; ן¿½twas vain to try.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The try was very lame;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, thoן¿½ I wouldnן¿½t show it, I
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was frightened, all the same.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Sheן¿½s mashed,ן¿½ said Jack, ן¿½I do not doubt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But ן¿½tis a lonely place;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And then you see it might turn out
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A breach of promise case.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He flogged the horse until it jibbed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And stood as one resigned,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And then he struck the road and ran
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And left the cart behind.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now, Jack and I since infancy
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Had shared our joys and cares,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And so I was resolved that we
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Should share each otherן¿½s scares.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We raced each other all the way
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And never slept that night,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And when we told the tale next day
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They said that we were, intoxicated.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Lawson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Lawson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
